segments.s257-g161: وزان is a connective meaning 'and from that/there'; ژنده is an adjective meaning 'ragged' modifying پیلان (elephants); پیلان is the plural of پیل 'elephant'; جنگی here qualifies the elephants as 'warlike' or 'for war' (i.e. battle‑elephants). The word order is typical older Persian: connective + adjective + noun + adjective/epithet.
segments.s257-g162: بیاراسته is being used adjectivally ('unadorned' / 'not decorated') describing the state of those elephants; از introduces a source 'from/of'; در here means 'door/gate' (درِ شهریار = the king's gate/entrance); شهریار is 'the king'. The phrase parses as a prepositional phrase 'unadorned from the king’s gate'.
segments.s257-g163: نهادند is the 3rd‑person plural past of نهادن 'they placed/put'; بر is the preposition 'on/upon'; پشتشان is 'their backs' (پشت + ـشان); تخت زر = 'throne (takhte) of gold' (تخت 'throne/seat' + زر 'gold'). Word order is verb + prepositional phrase + object in this clause.
segments.s257-g164: نشستنگه is a noun meaning 'seat/place to sit' (a seat or throne platform); شاه is 'the king' and is the possessor/beneficiary of the seat; با is the preposition 'with'; زیب 'beauty' and فر 'royal glory/splendour' are coordinated with و. Together this yields 'a seat for the king, with beauty and splendour.'
segments.s257-g165: به introduces the indirect object 'to Goudarz' (i.e. 'he said to Goudarz'); گودرز is the proper name; فرمود is a formal past‑tense verb 'he commanded/said'; تا introduces the subordinate clause of command/purpose ('that/in order that'); بر نشست is 'to sit upon' or 'sit on' (بر + نشست where نشست is the verb 'to sit'). Note classical Persian often uses تا + verb to mark an ordered or purposive clause.
segments.s257-g166: بر آن تخت زر is a locative phrase 'on that golden throne' (بر = on, آن = that, تخت زر = golden throne); از بر پیل مست is a locational/source phrase 'from/on the back of the drunken elephant' where از + بر here indicate position 'upon/from the back of'; پیل is 'elephant' and مست 'drunk/intoxicated' describes the elephant. The clause therefore specifies where he was to sit — on that throne, on the back of the intoxicated elephant.
segments.s257-g167: برانگیخت is a past tense verb 'it/they roused' or 'they stirred'; پیلان is 'the elephants' as the affected/agentive subject; و is 'and'; برخاست is 'rose' or 'arose'; گرد here is the noun 'dust' (گرد = dust) that is said to have 'arisen'. The clause thus describes the action (elephants stirred) and its consequence (dust rose).
segments.s257-g168: مر is the dative/clitic 'to me' (me); آن را is the direct object 'that' with the object marker را; به introduces the frame 'as/to' and links to نیک اختری 'auspicious star' (نیک = good/auspicious, اختری = star‑like/stellar); یاد کرد is the compound 'remembered/mentioned' (یاد 'remembrance' + کرد 'did' used to form 'remarked/mentioned/remembered'). Together it reads 'he mentioned that to me as an auspicious star/omen.'
segments.s257-g169: که is a subordinating conjunction meaning 'so that' or 'that'. از جان پیران is a prepositional phrase 'from the lives/souls of the elders' (از + noun جان + plural پیران). برآریم is a 1st-person plural subjunctive/optative form ('we might/let us raise, send up') placed before the object دود by poetic word order; دود is the direct object 'smoke'.
segments.s257-g170: بر آن سان is a fixed comparative phrase 'in that manner' or 'just so'. که introduces the comparative clause that follows. گرد here is the noun 'dust' (not the preposition), پی means 'foot/track' (here 'foot of'), پیل is 'elephant', and بود is the past copula 'was' — so گرد پی پیل بود = 'dust was about the elephant's foot/feet'.
segments.s257-g41: چو is a simile/comparative particle here ('like/as'). It precedes each name to introduce a list of exemplars; the phrase strings names in apposition without an explicit copula, a typical poetic listing. The coordinating و links the names; the final noun (گیو) completes the first group.
segments.s257-g42: Again چو begins another ‘like …’ unit. با‑style epithets or additional names follow with و as simple coordination; the final word نیو appears as a name or epithet attached to رهام (no explicit grammatical marker), so the group reads as a coordinated list of persons.
segments.s257-g43: چو before طوس and again before رستم marks parallel similes ('like Tous, like Rostam'). The noun phrase یل پهلوان follows رستم in apposition: two nouns stacked for emphasis ('a hero, a champion'), a common poetic appositional pattern where epithets follow the proper name.
segments.s257-g44: Two proper names are coordinated by و, followed by a two‑word epithet شیر دمان where شیر is a metaphorical noun ('lion'). دمان here functions as the second element of a binomial epithet (literary/poetic), forming a compound descriptor attached to the preceding names.
segments.s257-g45: دگر here means 'other/another' (introducing an additional group). The sequence names بیژن and گیو are coordinated, and با introduces گستهم as a comitative ('with Gostaham'), so grammatically this is another cluster of persons joined to the company.
segments.s257-g46: چو/چون again function as comparative markers ('like'); گرگین, زنگه and گژدهم are coordinated personal names (or name‑like epithets). The slight variation چون instead of چو is stylistic/metrical; overall the phrase continues the catalogue of warriors by simile.
segments.s257-g47: جز introduces an exception ('apart from/except'). این نامداران is the noun phrase 'these famed ones', and لشکر همه places 'army' before the quantifier همه ('all/the rest'), a normal Persian order meaning 'the whole army' or 'the rest of the host'.
segments.s257-g48: که introduces a relative clause modifying 'لشکر همه' ('which/who'), and بودند is the past plural copula 'they were'. شاه جهان را is the object/topic phrase ('the king of the world' – here 'the king'), with را marking the definite noun, and رمه is the predicate noun meaning 'a throng/host' describing what that army amounted to.
segments.s257-g113: This is a nominal phrase: سواران (plural head noun 'horsemen') is modified by the following phrase شایسته کارزار ('worthy of battle'). In Persian poetry the ezāfe is often omitted; شایسته governs کارزار as its object, yielding 'horsemen worthy of battle' or 'battle-worthy riders.'
segments.s257-g114: ببر is an imperative 'take/lead' addressing the addressee; تا introduces a purpose clause and is followed by the subjunctive/optative برآری ('you may accomplish' / 'you may bring about'). ز دشمن دمار is the object of that verb in inverted, poetic order: 'to bring ruin from/upon the enemy.'
segments.s257-g115: به + proper name functions as an indirect object or addressee ('to Ashkash'); بفرمود is the honorific past of گفتن used as 'he commanded/said.' تا introduces the following purpose or content clause governed by this command; سی هزار is the numeral quantifying the following units.
segments.s257-g116: This is a nominal list of troop-types quantified by the preceding numeral: دمنده ('trumpet/blast blowers') and نیزه گزار (a compound 'spear‑bearers') possibly apposed to or qualified by هژبران (a name or epithet). Persian compounds and appositions often stack like this in epic verse; the whole is the object of the command 'bring.'
segments.s257-g117: برد here functions as a past-tense verb 'he carried/bore' (poetic placement before its object); سوی خوارزم is a directional complement 'toward Khwarezm', and کوس بزرگ is the (postposed) object 'the great war-drum.' Poetic word order places direction and object after the verb.
segments.s257-g118: سپاهی is the noun 'army'; به + کردار introduces a manner phrase 'in the manner/behaviour of' (به used adverbially), followed by درنده گرگ ('ferocious wolf') as a simile. The phrase describes the army's conduct: 'an army behaving like a predatory wolf.'
segments.s257-g119: زند is a poetic present/past-form of 'to strike/ smite' (here 'he struck') and takes the prepositional phrase بر در شهر خوارزم ('upon the gate of the city of Khwarezm') as its locative/complement. گاه functions as a temporal adverb 'then' / 'at that moment' linking to what follows.
segments.s257-g120: ابا شیده appears as an epithet (a compound modifier) describing the following noun رزم زن ('battle‑woman' or 'one who fights'). کینه خواه is another compound adjective: کینه ('hatred, grudges') + خواه ('desirer/seeker') = 'vengeful' or 'seeking revenge.' Together these modifiers describe the warrior who acts (the subject of the previous verb).
segments.s257-g33: This clause begins with the noun phrase 'سیه مار' (black snake) followed by the relative/locative particle 'کو' ('where' / 'that where'), then the object marker 'را' attached to 'سر' (head). The verb 'آید' (comes/appears) is in the present/regular form and governs the following imperative 'بکوب' (strike), which is a direct command addressed to the listener. Word order is topic — relative phrase — object — verb — imperative; in effect: 'When the black snake's head comes out, strike (it).' 'را' highlights 'سر' as definite/direct object of the implied action (bکوب applies to the head/creature).
segments.s257-g34: 'ز سوراخ' is a prepositional phrase ('from the hole') with the poetic form 'ز' for 'از'. 'پیچان شود' is a verbal phrase: 'پیچان' (twisting, present participial adjective) plus 'شود' (become / comes to be), so together they describe the snake's motion — 'it writhes/twists.' 'سوی چوب' ('toward the stick') gives the direction. The clause therefore describes manner and direction: 'it twists out of the hole toward the stick.'
segments.s257-g35: The clause opens with the conditional/time marker 'چو' ('when' / 'if') followed by the subject 'خسرو' (Khosrow). The prepositional phrase 'به بیداد' means 'by/through injustice' (an ablative/causal modifier). The noun phrase 'کارد درخت' places 'کارد' (knife) next to 'درخت' (tree); syntactically this is the object or oblique phrase affected by the action in the next segment — i.e. 'turning the knife on the tree' or 'the knife against the tree'. Word order here is archaic/poetic (modifier after the verb to come), so this segment sets the circumstance: 'When Khosrow, through injustice, (applies) the knife to the tree...'.
segments.s257-g36: 'بگردد' is a present/future-tense verbal form from گردیدن ('to turn') meaning 'will turn' or 'is turned'; here it expresses the consequence/result. 'بر او' ('against him' / 'upon him') marks the target of the ensuing effect. 'پادشاهی و تخت' ('kingship and throne') are the nominative/subject nouns that 'turn' — i.e. they will be turned against him. Overall this segment gives the result clause: 'kingship and throne will turn against him.'
segments.s257-g37: 'همه موبدان' ('all the mōbeds') is the direct object group; the definite/object marker 'را' marks them as definite. 'بر خویش' is a reflexive/directional phrase meaning 'to himself' (literally 'upon his own'), formed from 'خوی' + possessive 'ش'. 'خواند' is the past-tense verb 'he called/summoned.' Word order is object — directional ('to himself') — verb, a common poetic arrangement for emphasis: 'He summoned all the priests to himself.'
segments.s257-g38: This clause continues the action: 'شنیده سخن' is a collocation meaning 'renowned/learned speech' (literally 'heard speech'), 'پیش ایشان' means 'before them/in their presence', and 'براند' here functions as a verb meaning to present/utter (an older or poetic usage related to راندن). Thus the clause says he put forward or spoke well-known/learned words before them. Word order: adjective+noun — prepositional phrase — verb.
segments.s257-g39: The verb 'نشستند' (they sat) is a past-tense, 3rd-person plural verb introducing a group action. 'با شاه ایران' ('with the king of Iran') names the person present. 'به راز' literally 'to/for secret' means 'in counsel' or 'in private/confidential council.' The clause therefore states that these people 'sat with the king of Iran in counsel.' Note: the lemma senses supplied for 'نشستند' did not match the verb's normal meaning; here it is the standard 'they sat/met.'
segments.s257-g40: This is a noun phrase listing those present: 'بزرگان' (the great ones), 'فرزانه' (the wise/sages), and 'رزم ساز' (literally 'war-makers' or 'those who make/prepare battle'). The conjunction 'و' links the descriptors. 'رزم ساز' is a compound in which 'ساز' functions as 'maker/creator' or 'one who readies' (here 'maker of war'); word order is attributive: major class — qualities.
segments.s257-g89: This phrase lists two pieces of armour introduced by the preposition به (here instrumental/benefactive sense: 'with/by'), joined by و. چو introduces a simile 'like/as' modifying what follows (کوه). Word order is preposition + noun (به برگستوان), repeated for emphasis (به جوشن), then the simile clause (چو کوه) that will attach to the verb in the next segment.
segments.s257-g90: شدند is a past plural verb 'became' (شد + -ند). انجمن لشکری is a noun phrase 'military assembly/meeting' (انجمن modified by لشکری). همگروه functions adverbially/adjectivally to indicate they are of the same group or gathered together. The whole clause states the result: they became a united military assembly.
segments.s257-g91: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. شد is the past verbal root 'became/happened' marking the completion. کار لشکر is a genitive phrase 'the army's affair/business' and همه ساخته is a predicate participle phrase 'all made/completed' (ساخته used as past participle). Together: 'when the army's affair was all completed'.
segments.s257-g92: وز here functions as a prepositional/archaic connector ('from/among' or 'of'), followed by ایشان 'them'; دل شاه is the possessor phrase 'the king's heart'. پرداخته is a past participle of پرداختن meaning 'turned/taken/been given' (here passive/resultative: 'the king's heart was turned toward them'). The clause expresses the king's emotional inclination after the army's business was done.
segments.s257-g93: نخستین is an ordinal/adjective 'first'. از آن لشکر نامدار is a genitive chain 'from that renowned army' (از + demonstrative آن + noun + adjective). The phrase sets up a subsection of the army that will be specified next.
segments.s257-g94: سواران is the plural noun 'horsemen/riders'. شمشیر زن is a compound noun where شمشیر 'sword' is combined with زن (from زدن 'to strike') to mean 'sword-strikers' = 'swordsmen' (here an agentive use of زن). The numeral phrase 'سی هزار' follows in the line (thirty thousand) specifying their number.
segments.s257-g95: گزین کرد is a compound verb 'chose/selected' (گزین + کرد), with the finite verb first followed by the subject خسرو (a common poetic word order V‑S). به رستم سپرد is a second verb phrase 'entrusted to Rostam' where به marks the recipient and سپرد is the past 'handed/entrusted'. The two coordinated actions describe the king's decision and act of entrusting.
segments.s257-g96: بدو is an enclitic prepositional pronoun 'to him'. گفت here functions as the verb 'said' (the lemma senses list is nominal but context shows speech). کای is a vocative particle 'O' (archaic/epic). نامبردار addresses the addressee as 'renowned/celebrated'. گرد at the end is a verbal/imperative or archaic verbal form ('now, go about/assemble' or 'become/turn'), used as part of the direct address; its exact nuance is poetic/archaic.
segments.s257-g129: This is a nominal apposition: title + name + epithet appear in sequence without a verb. سپهدار is a title 'commander', followed by the proper name گودرز, then the archaic epithet کشوادگان attached to the same person.
segments.s257-g130: A simple coordinated noun phrase: همه (all/everyone) modifies the following plural nouns; پهلوانان و آزادگان are coordinated (heroes/champions and the free men). Word order is typical Persian: quantifier + noun(s).
segments.s257-g131: نشستند is a past tense, 3rd-person plural of 'نشستن' (they sat/mounted). The locative phrase بر زین (on the saddle) follows the verb, and به فرمان شاه ('by/at the king's command') expresses the cause/agent of the action. The subject is omitted because it was given in the previous segment.
segments.s257-g132: A short prepositional phrase giving position: سپهدار گودرز is the title+name (subject/agent), and پیش سپاه means 'in front of the host/army' where پیش functions as a preposition/adverb 'before/in front of'. The sequence indicates Goudarz is leading the army.
segments.s257-g133: به گودرز فرمود uses the dative preposition به to mark the indirect object ('to Goudarz') and فرمود (he said/ordered) as the reporting verb. پس is a discourse particle 'then', and شهریار (the king) appears after the verb as the subject—Persian allows subject-verb inversion for emphasis or rhythm.
segments.s257-g134: This is a temporal subordinate clause introduced by چو = 'when'. رفتی is 2nd-person past ('you go/went') addressing Goudarz; کمر بسته is a participial/adjectival phrase 'with belt girded' (literally 'waist tied'), placed before the noun کارزار (battle) to describe the state when he goes. The clause means 'when you go, girded for combat, to the battlefield.'
segments.s257-g135: نگر is an imperative/imperatival verb meaning 'see to it' or 'take care'. تا introduces the purpose/result ('so that' / 'lest'); نیازی here is the noun 'need' (نیاز) and is used with به to form 'need for ...'; بیداد is 'injustice/oppression' and دست ('hand') here means 'power/help'. Grammatically this chunk is 'Make sure so that there is no need for the hand of oppression.'
segments.s257-g136: نگردانی is a negative jussive/imperative form (ن‑ + گردانی) meaning 'do not cause/make' or 'do not turn into' derived from گردانیدن. ایوان آباد is a noun + adjective 'the (well‑)prosperous hall/palace', and پست is an adjective 'base/despicable' qualifying those who would occupy it. Together the phrase commands 'do not make the prosperous palace serve the base/despicable.'
segments.s257-g65: کسی کو functions as an indefinite relative ('anyone who' / 'one who'); کو here is an archaic/poetic relative particle. بساید is a present/subjunctive‑style verb meaning 'holds/keeps' or 'is able to hold' and takes عنان و رکیب (reins and the mount) as its objects; the phrase lists the two things that are being restrained/controlled.
segments.s257-g66: نباید is an impersonal negative modal ('one should not' / 'it ought not to be that'); که introduces the subordinate clause whose verb is یابد ('find' / 'come upon') in the present-subjunctive/indicative sense. به خانه is a locative phrase ('at/to the house/home') and شکیب is a noun meaning 'patience' (here 'patience/endurance'), the thing that will not be found.
segments.s257-g67: بفرمود is the honorific/past‑tense third‑person verb 'he said/commanded'. کز is a poetic contraction meaning 'from' here, introducing the origins (روم 'Rum/Byzantium' and هندوان 'the Indians'); وز is the fused form of و + ز ('and from'), an archaic/poetic linking of the two origins.
segments.s257-g68: سواران جنگی is a noun phrase 'war‑riders' (سواران 'riders/horsemen' modified by جنگی 'warlike/military'). گزیده is a participle/adjective 'chosen/selected'. گوان is an archaic adverbial/postpositive particle meaning 'like/so' or marking quality; together the phrase describes 'selected, warlike horsemen.'
segments.s257-g69: دلیران گردنکش is a noun phrase with two post‑nominal adjectives/epithets: دلیران 'the brave ones' and گردنکش 'rebellious/headstrong', forming a compound description. از introduces the origin/source 'from', and تازیان names that source ('the Arabs').
segments.s257-g70: بسیچیده is a perfective/adjectival form meaning 'arrayed/abundantly gathered' (poetic for 'marshaled'). جنگ here is 'battle/war'. شیر ژیان is a poetic compound: شیر 'lion' modifies ژیان 'lives/people' to convey 'lion‑hearted men' or 'like lions'—a figurative epithet for fierce fighters.
segments.s257-g71: کمربسته is an adjectival/passive participle 'girded' (i.e., armed/ready). خواهند is a future/auxiliary in old poetic usage ('there will be / they will'), so the phrase means 'there will be three hundred thousand girded (men)'; word order places the numeral before the verb/auxiliary for emphasis.
segments.s257-g72: ز دشت is a poetic locative 'from the plain' (ز = 'from'). سواران is 'horsemen/riders'. نیزه گزار is a compound meaning 'spear‑bearers' or 'lancers' (نیزه 'spear' + گزار/گذار as an agentive element), an archaic spelling/formation used to denote those who carry spears.
segments.s257-g1: پس is a temporal/adverbial particle meaning “then.” آگاهی (literally “information/news”) is the subject noun and آمد is the past tense verb “came/reached.” به + پیروز شاه is a prepositional phrase marking the recipient (“to the victorious king”); adjective پیروز precedes the noun شاه in the classical word order.
segments.s257-g2: که introduces a reported clause (“that …”). آمد is the verb “came” and is placed before the rest of the clause in this poetic inversion. ز توران به ایران is the path/movement phrase “from Turan into Iran,” and سپاه at the end names the moving entity (“an army/host”).
segments.s257-g3: A string of epithets precedes the proper name: جفاپیشه and بدگوهر are descriptive adjectives/epithets modifying افراسیاب. There is no copula; this is the conventional poetic way to present a named character with attributes.
segments.s257-g4: ز کینه is an ablative/causal prepositional phrase “from/because of hatred.” نیابد is the negative present/habitual form of یافتن used here in the narrative sense “did not find / could not enjoy.” شب و روز (“night and day”) is an adverbial phrase inserted before the object خواب (“sleep”), which is the thing not found.
segments.s257-g5: برآورد here is a verbal phrase meaning roughly “raise/lift” (used poetically with سر), placed before the auxiliary; خواهد is the modal auxiliary indicating desire/future and همی is the classical continuous/emphatic particle. سر ز ننگ is the object phrase “his head from shame” (i.e. to rid himself of disgrace). The order (verb before auxiliary/particle) is a poetic inversion.
segments.s257-g6: ز هر سو is a prepositional phrase “from every side.” فرستاد is the past-tense verb “sent/dispatched” and takes لشکر as its direct object; بجنگ (ب + جنگ) is a purpose infinitive meaning “to battle” or “into the fight.” The sequence uses poetic word order but reads naturally as “he sent troops from every side to the battle.”
segments.s257-g7: همی is the habitual/continuous particle “continuously/ever.” زهر is the object “poison.” ساید here is a poetic/archaic verb meaning “he smeared/daubed/rubbed on” (from the root ساییدن); it takes به نوک سنان (“on the tips of the spears”) as the prepositional goal. The verb form and word order are characteristic of classical Persian poetry.
segments.s257-g8: که introduces a result/purpose clause (“so that…”). تابد is from تابیدن used here in the sense “to turn/bend” (i.e. the reins turn); مگر functions in classical usage to mark the purposive/optative sense “that/so that” (not its common modern sense “except”), and سوی ایران عنان means “the reins toward Iran” (عنان = reins/bridle, used figuratively for direction). The clause therefore says the action was done so the reins would be turned toward Iran.
segments.s257-g121: This is a simple past clause with the verb داد (gave) at the end, typical Persian SOV order: direct object سپاه چهارم (the fourth army) precedes the prepositional indirect object به گودرز (to Goudarz). The subject is omitted (third‑person actor understood from context) and داد is the past 3rd‑person form 'gave'.
segments.s257-g122: چه مایه is an exclamatory/adverbial phrase meaning 'what a quantity / how much' modifying the following nouns. ورا is an older object clitic (= به او, 'to him') placed before the nouns پند و اندرز ('advice and counsel'); the verb داد (gave) closes the clause. The pairing پند و اندرز are near‑synonyms joined by و for emphasis.
segments.s257-g123: که introduces a relative clause ('who/that'). رو here has the sense 'toward/turning to' and with با بزرگان ایران به هم means 'joining/going together with the great ones of Iran' (با + plural بزرگان = 'with the great men', به هم = 'together'). Word order places the prepositional phrase between رو and the particle به هم for poetic rhythm.
segments.s257-g124: چو ... چون ... is a series of similes ('as/like ... and like ...'); the items that follow are personal names (classical Iranian heroes) enumerated for comparison. The conjunctions و simply link the names; the phrase functions as examples of the 'great men' just mentioned.
segments.s257-g125: This segment is a nominal series: a list of proper names separated by و. In classical Persian poems names are often given without commas; the sequence functions as a single coordinated noun phrase (several heroes).
segments.s257-g126: Another coordinated phrase of names/titles. گرازه appears immediately before the title سپهدار, so the noun + title reads as 'Garazeh, the sepahdar (commander)'; و links that to رهام نیو, another name with an epithet. سپهدار is a title used after a name in apposition.
segments.s257-g127: بفرمود is an honorific past‑tense verb 'he commanded/ordered' (narrative third person). It governs the following infinitival action بستن کمرشان ('to bind/gird their waists') where کمرشان is the possessed noun (کمر + شان 'their'). به جنگ specifies purpose 'for (the) battle'.
segments.s257-g128: سوی + noun indicates direction ('toward'); رزم توران is a genitive phrase 'the battle of Turan'. شدن here functions as an intransitive narrative verb 'they set out / proceeded' (a classical/poetic usage where the form appears without a modern personal ending). بی‌درنگ is an adverbial modifier 'without delay / immediately'.
segments.s257-g57: بزد is a 3rd-person singular past-tense verb ('he struck/struck at') with an implied subject (typical of classical Persian). مهره در جام is the object phrase ('a bead / bead in the cup') and sits immediately after the verb here by poetic inversion; بر پشت پیل is a locative prepositional phrase ('upon the elephant's back') specifying where the action took place.
segments.s257-g58: زمین را is marked with the object/definite marker را; تو گفتی is a 2nd-person past ('you said/you declared') addressing someone. براندود is an adjectival/passive‑like participial form meaning 'smeared/coated', and نیل here names the substance ('indigo'), so the clause reads literally 'you said the earth (was) smeared with indigo.'
segments.s257-g59: هوا نیلگون شد is a simple verbal clause: هوا ('the air/sky') is the subject, نیلگون ('indigo/blue') is a predicate adjective and شد is the past of شدن ('became'). زمین رنگ رنگ places زمین ('the earth/ground') followed by the reduplicated adjective رنگ رنگ ('many‑colored / of varied colors') — poetic repetition to emphasize variegation.
segments.s257-g60: دلیران لشکر is a noun phrase 'the brave ones of the host/army' (دلیران modifies لشکر). به سان is a fixed comparative ('like, as') and پلنگ is the simile head 'leopard' — together 'the brave of the army like leopards.'
segments.s257-g61: به here introduces an instrumental/associative phrase ('with / having'); چنگ is used in its non‑musical sense 'claw/handgrip' (i.e. 'a grasp' or 'in hand'), اندرون here means 'inward/among them' or 'inside (their ranks)', and گرز is 'mace/club' — so the cluster describes them 'with a mace in hand.' و links to the next phrase; دل پر ز کین is an idiom: دل ('heart') plus پر ('full (of)') + ز کین ('with hatred'), meaning 'hearts full of hatred.'
segments.s257-g62: ز گردان is a prepositional phrase ('from the ranks/host'); چو introduces a simile ('like/as'); دریای جوشان is a compound 'boiling/seething sea' (دریای = sea, جوشان = boiling/seething), and زمین follows as the locus — poetically 'from the ranks the earth was like a seething sea' (i.e. the massed troops made the ground seem to seethe).
segments.s257-g63: خروشی برآمد is a verbal clause meaning 'a roar/clamor issued forth' (خروشی = roar/clamor, برآمد = rose/issued). ز درگاه شاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the court/threshold of the king' — درگاه here denotes the royal presence or throne‑room.
segments.s257-g64: که‌ای is a vocative/exclamatory opening ('O' / 'O ye'), addressing the assembled heroes; پهلوانان ایران سپاه is a condensed vocative phrase 'heroes / champions — army of Iran' (پهلوانان = champions, ایران modifies سپاه as the nation whose army it is). The clause is direct address from the court.
segments.s257-g81: This is a fronted temporal/adverbial phrase. به شبگیر places the scene 'at nightfall' and گاه is an adverb meaning 'sometimes' or 'occasionally'; خروش خروس is a possessive noun phrase ('the cry of the rooster') with the possessor (خروس) following the noun of sound (خروش), a common poetic order.
segments.s257-g82: ز هر سوی برخاست is a short clause 'rose from every direction' with برخاست (past, 3rd pl./3rd sg. in context) as the main verb and ز هر سوی as a prepositional adverbial of source/direction. آوای کوس is the subject (literally 'the voice/sound of the drum'); poetic inversion places the verb before the subject for emphasis.
segments.s257-g83: بزرگان هر کشوری is a noun phrase where هر کشوری ('of every country') post-modifies بزرگان ('the great ones, magnates'). با سپاه is an accompanying phrase 'with the army/with their troops' attached to that noun phrase.
segments.s257-g84: نهادند is a past plural verb 'they placed/put' (here idiomatically 'bowed'). سر is the direct object 'head' and سوی درگاه شاه is a directional phrase 'toward the king's court/presence.' The literal structure 'نهادند سر سوی درگاه شاه' corresponds to 'they laid/bent their heads toward the royal court.'
segments.s257-g85: در گنجهای کهن is a locative/locution 'in the old treasuries' (گنجهای here plural), followed by باز کرد, a compound meaning 'opened' (باز 'open' + کرد 'did' forming the past tense 'opened'). The implied subject (he/the king) is omitted as is common in Persian.
segments.s257-g86: سپه را marks the army with the object marker را and functions as the beneficiary/recipient in the clause. دادن is the verbal infinitive 'to give' used with آغاز کرد ('began') to form 'began to give,' and درم is the thing given (dirhams). آغاز کرد is past 'he began,' so the whole phrase reads 'he began to give dirhams to the army.'
segments.s257-g87: همه لشکر is the topical noun phrase 'the whole army.' از گنج و دینار شاه is a source/genitive phrase 'from the king's treasure and dinars' indicating where the wealth came from; poetic word order places the source after the topic.
segments.s257-g88: به سر بر نهادند is an idiomatic verbal cluster 'they put on (their) heads' (نهادند past plural 'they placed/put', بر = 'on', به سر = 'on the head'). گوهر کلاه is the object 'jeweled hat(s)' (literally 'gem [of] hat' meaning hats adorned with gems). The verb governs the object and the dative/locative phrase together.
segments.s257-g49: This phrase means 'the king said thus to the champions.' In classical Persian the verb can come before the subject, so گفت (said) appears before شاه (the king). ابا here functions as an archaic/poetic prepositional or vocative element addressing the champions (effectively 'to/with the champions'); چنین is an adverb 'thus' modifying گفت.
segments.s257-g50: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that'). ترکان is the subject 'the Turks.' همی before the verb gives a habitual/continuous sense ('regularly, always'). رزم جویند is a light-verb construction: رزم (battle) + جویند (they seek) = 'they seek battle' (present/habitual). و گاه begins a new phrase 'and at times...' that the next line will complete.
segments.s257-g51: چو here is a temporal conjunction 'when.' سپه کرد is a causative/construct meaning 'made/raised an army' (سپه = army, کرد = 'made/caused'); the subject is دشمن ('the enemy'). شد + predicate (تیز چنگ) uses شد as a linking verb 'became' — تیز (sharp) modifies چنگ (claw), a metaphorical phrase meaning 'became fierce/with sharpened talons.'
segments.s257-g52: بباید is a modal 'it is necessary / one must.' بسیچید is the verbal noun/infinitival idea 'to muster/gather' (here used with بباید to express obligation). ما را is the object 'us' with the object marker را; به جنگ 'for war' marks purpose/target. The clause therefore says it is necessary to muster us for battle.
segments.s257-g53: بفرمود is an honorific past-tense verb 'he commanded/ordered.' تا introduces purpose/result ('so that/that'), here linking the command to the following actions. بوق با گاودم is an instrumental phrase: بوق 'the horn' and با گاودم 'with a cow's tail' (i.e. the horn fitted or sounded using a cow‑tail implement).
segments.s257-g54: دمیدند is a past plural verb 'they blew' (the horn). و joins the next action: بستند 'they fastened/closed.' رویینه خم is a compound noun phrase: رویینه 'armoured/iron' modifying خم 'curve/bend' (here a curved iron piece or cuirass/cover); grammatically it is the object of بستند.
segments.s257-g55: از ... به marks movement 'from the iwan to the field.' خرامید is a past tense verb 'strode/pranced proudly' describing the king's manner; note classical word order places the subject شاه after the verb. The whole segment reports the king's proud entrance into the arena.
segments.s257-g56: بیاراستند is past plural 'they adorned/arrayed (him).' از بر is a prepositional phrase 'from/on/atop' (old-fashioned ordering). پیل is 'elephant' and گاه here names the spot/place associated with the elephant ('elephant‑place' or platform); together the phrase describes arranging the king on the elephant or its platform. (گاه has a locative sense here in context.)
segments.s257-g25: سپاهی is the head noun 'army/soldiers'. به کردار + noun expresses manner ('in the manner/conduct of'); کردار is 'conduct/behavior' and is followed by پیلان مست, where پیلان (elephants) is modified by the adjective مست (drunk/intoxicated) that comes after the noun as is normal in Persian.
segments.s257-g26: که introduces a relative/result clause ('whose/that'); با marks the means/instrument ('by/with') and جنگ ایشان is 'their fighting/war' (ایشان = they). شود is 3rd-person present/future-like 'becomes/happens', and کوه پست is a predicate nominal ('a mountain low' = 'a mountain is laid low'), with the adjective پست following the noun.
segments.s257-g27: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is the simple past 'he heard'. گفتار کاراگهان is a possessed noun phrase 'the speech of Karagahan' (noun + possessor), object of بشنید; the verb precedes its object here as common in poetry.
segments.s257-g28: پراندیشه is an adjectival/compound 'full of thought, pensive' placed before the verb for emphasis. بنشست is the past 'sat down'. شاه جهان is the title 'king of the world' (شاه = king, جهان = world) with the possessor following the head noun.
segments.s257-g29: به + proper name marks the indirect object ('to Karagahan'). گفت is the past verb 'said' (here used to introduce speech). کای is the vocative particle 'O', and بخردان is a vocative plural 'wise ones' (بخرد as adjective/noun 'wise').
segments.s257-g30: من is the first-person subject 'I'. ایدون is an archaic/time adverb meaning roughly 'now/recently' (used to qualify the following hearing) and is placed before the verbal form. شنیدستم is a past-perfect/simple past compound meaning 'I heard / I was told'. از marks source 'from' and موبدان is the agent/source 'the mobeds (Zoroastrian priests)'.
segments.s257-g31: که introduces the subordinate clause ('that/for when'). چون is temporal 'when'. ماه ترکان is a possessive phrase 'the moon of the Turks' (metaphorical). برآید is 3rd-person present/future-like 'rises/appears', and بلند modifies it as 'high/lofty'.
segments.s257-g32: ز (poetic form of از) marks source 'from'. خورشید is 'sun' (here likely metaphorical). ایرانش is 'Iran' with the enclitic -ش marking possession/association ('its Iran'/'Iran' in relation to the subject/context). آید is 'comes' and گزند is the noun 'harm'—together 'harm comes from ...'.
segments.s257-g9: This is a noun phrase: سواران جنگی = 'war-riders' (noun + adjectival modifier). چو before the numeral functions like 'چون/چو' = 'like / amounting to', giving an approximate number: 'like three hundred thousand'. Word order (noun + adjective + چو + numeral) is typical in epic Persian.
segments.s257-g10: به جیحون marks the destination ('to/into the Jeyhun'). همی is the older progressive/emphatic particle marking ongoing or prospective action. کرد together with خواهد and گذار form a periphrastic verbal sequence meaning 'would cause to pass / would pass' (کرد here as a verbal element 'do/bring about', خواهد = future auxiliary 'will/would', گذار the lexical root 'pass/let pass'). The clause thus means they would pass into the Jeyhun.
segments.s257-g11: سپاهی 'an army' is the head noun; که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'. هنگام ننگ و نبرد = 'at the time of shame and of battle' (هنگام + noun phrase), so this locates the event temporally: 'in the hour of dishonour and fighting'.
segments.s257-g12: ز here is the classical poetic preposition 'from'; جیحون is the source. به گردون = 'to the sky/heavens' (به + گردون as the goal). برآورد is a past/preterite verbal form meaning 'raised, brought up' (here used transitively), and گرد is the noun 'dust' (a cloud of dust) — together the verb + object mean 'he raised a cloud of dust from the Jeyhun up to the heavens', i.e. figuratively 'he lifted an army from the Jeyhun as if up to the sky.'
segments.s257-g13: دلیران is the plural noun 'the brave' (warriors). به درگاه افراسیاب uses به with درگاه to indicate location/position 'at/to the court (presence) of Afrasiab' (درگاه = royal court/threshold). The phrase names who is sleepless.
segments.s257-g14: ز introduces the cause/reason 'from/because of'. بانگ تبیره = 'the cry/sound (بانگ) of the tabireh/announcement' (تبیره here 'explanation/announcement' used as the source of an alarm). نیابند is the negative present plural of یافتن (یابیدن) meaning 'they do not find' (so 'they cannot find'), and خواب is the object 'sleep' — together: 'they do not find sleep' (i.e. they cannot sleep because of the cry).
segments.s257-g15: ز آوای شیپور = 'from the sound of the trumpet' (ز = 'from/because of', آوای = 'sound/voice' in the genitive, شیپور = 'trumpet'). و زخم درای links 'and' plus a compound: زخم = 'wound/blow' and درای, used adjectivally ('possessing/possessed') is a poetic epithet modifying زخم; together this phrase names another cause (the trumpet and a mighty wound/blow).
segments.s257-g16: تو گویی here is a parenthetical/classical usage: گویی (from گفتن) functions like 'you would say' or 'it seems' (introducing a simile or apparent state). برآید is the present/subjunctive-like form 'arises/appears'. همی is the older progressive/emphatic particle. دل ز جای = 'the heart from its place' (دل = heart; ز جای = from its place) — together: 'you seem as if the heart would rise from its place.'
segments.s257-g73: هر آن کو is an archaic relative phrase meaning 'whoever' (هر = 'any/whichever', آن + کو ~ 'that who'); چهل روزه را is a definite time-period phrase ('the forty days') with را marking it as definite/emphatic; نزد شاه means 'in the presence of the king' (نزد = 'before, in the presence of'). Together they set the subject and time/place ('whoever, within that forty-day period, before the king').
segments.s257-g74: نیاید is a negative present/jussive form meaning 'does not come / does not present himself'; نبیند is a negative present/future-like consequence 'will not see' (used here to state the result). The locative phrase به سر بر کلاه is inverted poetic word order for کلاه بر سر ('a hat on the head'). So the clause expresses the consequence: if one does not come, one will not see a hat on his head (i.e. will be deprived of rank/honor).
segments.s257-g75: پراگنده is an adjective 'scattered/widespread' placed before the location phrase; بر گرد کشور is a poetic prepositional phrase 'around/about the country'; سوار (here plural) 'riders/horsemen' is the noun being described. Word order is somewhat inverted (predicate/adjective before noun) as common in epic Persian.
segments.s257-g76: فرستاده is a nominal subject 'a messenger' (the verb is omitted in this compressed/poetic line); با نامه شهریار is a prepositional + genitive phrase 'with the king's letter' (نامه 'letter' modified by شهریار in possessive relation). The whole segment implies 'a messenger was sent with the king's letter.'
segments.s257-g77: دو هفته is a temporal phrase 'two weeks'; برآمد is a past/poetic verb 'arose/issued forth' — here it indicates that something happened after that time; به فرمان شاه is an agent/instrument phrase 'by the king's command'. The order places the time expression first, then the verb, then the instrument ('two weeks later, at the king's command it arose').
segments.s257-g78: بجنبید is a poetic/older-form verb meaning 'was roused / moved / set in motion' (here describing the army's action); در پادشاهی سپاه literally orders the locative then the subject: 'in the kingship/kingdom, the army' — i.e. 'the army throughout the kingdom.' The verb precedes its subject in this inversion common in epic diction.
segments.s257-g79: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' or 'out of'; لشکر همه کشور is a genitive/noun phrase 'the armies of the whole country' (لشکر = army, همه کشور = entire country); آمد is past 'came' and به جوش is an idiom 'into a boil/surge' meaning they were stirred up or inflamed. Together: 'from the country's armies there came a surge/boiling.'
segments.s257-g80: ز گیتی 'from the world' (ز = 'from'); بر آمد is the past verb 'rose up'; سراسر خروش is a compound 'throughout, an uproar' (سراسر = throughout, خروش = roar/uproar). The clause says that a tumult rose throughout the world — again inverted word order for poetic effect (verb before phrase).
segments.s257-g97: ره سیستان گیر و برکش پگاه functions as an imperative charge. ره سیستان (literally 'the Sistan road') is the direct object/destination; گیر is an imperative 'take/seize' (classical/poetic usage). و simply links two imperatives, and برکش پگاه is a second imperative phrase meaning 'raise/lift (yourself) at dawn' or 'set out at dawn' (پگاه = dawn/early morning). Word order is topical: route first, then imperative verbs and the temporal adverb.
segments.s257-g98: به هندوستان اندر آور سپاه is a continuation of the command: به هندوستان ('into/toward India') gives the destination; اندر آور is a verbal periphrasis with آور (from آوردن) 'bring/lead (in)' and اندر reinforcing 'into/within'; سپاه is the patient/thing brought = 'the army'. The verb phrase places the action of bringing/leading the army into India.
segments.s257-g99: ز غزنین برو تا به راه برین: ز غزنین ('from Ghazni') marks the starting point; برو is an imperative 'go/set out'; تا introduces a limit 'until/as far as'; به راه برین ('to the راهِ برین') names the destination/limit — برین is an elevated adjective here ('the lofty/exalted'), formed in the text as بر + ین. Word order places source, verb, then the endpoint introduced by تا.
segments.s257-g100: چو گردد ترا تاج و تخت و نگین is a temporal clause introduced by چو 'when'. گردد (from گشت/گردیدن) means 'becomes/happens' (3rd-person, potential/factual), and ترا (ت + را combined) = 'to you' (2nd-person object). تاج و تخت و نگین are the items that will come/become yours: 'the crown, the throne, and the gem.' The clause thus sets the condition/time for what follows.
segments.s257-g101: چو آن پادشاهی شود یکسره is another temporal/conditional clause: چو 'when', آن پادشاهی 'that kingship' as the subject, شود (شدن) 'becomes/is established' (3rd-person), and یکسره 'completely/utterly' modifies the verb. The clause means 'when that kingship becomes complete.'
segments.s257-g102: به آبشخور آید پلنگ و بره: به + مکان = 'to/at' the آبشخور (watering-place); آید (آمدن) 'comes' (3rd-person); پلنگ و بره are the nominative subjects 'the leopard and the lamb.' The juxtaposition is a poetic image: when kingship is complete, predator and prey are both drawn to the common watering-place.
segments.s257-g103: فرامرز را ده کلاه و نگین is an instruction with the indirect object fronted and marked by را: فرامرز را 'to Faramarz', followed by the goods 'ده کلاه و نگین' ('ten helmets and a gem'). In context the verb 'give' is understood (imperative ordering), so this phrase names the beneficiary and the items to be given.
segments.s257-g104: کسی کو بخواهد ز لشکر گزین is a relative/conditional continuation: کسی کو 'whoever/anyone who' (کسی = someone/anyone; کو is the classical relative), بخواهد (from خواستن) = 'wishes/wants' (subjunctive/future sense), and ز لشکر گزین = 'choose from the army' (ز = from, لشکر = army, گزین = 'choose' — here an instructive infinitive/imperative). The clause means 'let anyone who wants (to) choose from the army.'
segments.s257-g153: بدان سان is a fixed poetic phrase meaning “in that way / so”; here it is split into ب + دان + سان (prefix + element + manner‑word). شوم is a first‑person present/subjunctive/potential of شدن “to become” (here: “may I become / let me be”). کم modifies the speaker’s status (“small/insignificant”); the clause ends with تو فرمان دهی where دهی is the present subjunctive/indicative 2nd‑person of دادن “to give” (you give/you may give), so the whole clause means “may I be so (low) that you give the commands.”
segments.s257-g154: A balanced nominative sentence: تو شاه جهانداری literally ‘you [are] king, world‑ruler’. The copula is omitted (a standard Persian/poetic verbless equative). و links the clauses and introduces the speaker’s self‑description من رهی where رهی is a nominal form meaning ‘a wayfarer/wanderer’.
segments.s257-g155: برآمد is a past/perfect verb meaning “arose/issued forth” and precedes its noun object here (a common V‑initial poetic order): خروش ‘a cry/roar’. از + در (here در as ‘door, gate’) marks source: “from the champion’s door” (پهلوان in postposition marks possessor).
segments.s257-g156: ز (= از, poetic) marks cause/source: “by/from the cry (بانگ) of تبیره.” تبیره here functions as a war‑blast/trumpet‑cry in context (poetic vocabulary). زمین شد نوان uses شد “became” + نوان “new/young” to mean the earth was renewed or quickened by that cry.
segments.s257-g157: This clause places the locative phrase first for emphasis: به لشکرگه آمد دمادم سپاه = “the army came (آمد) into/onto the camp (به لشکرگه) continuously (دمادم).” دمادم is an adverb meaning “again and again / constantly.” Word order is poetic (locative + verb + adverb + subject).
segments.s257-g158: جهان شد uses شد “became” with جهان ‘the world’ as subject. ز گرد here means ‘by/from the round/circuit’ or ‘because of the throng’ of سواران سیاه — i.e. the circling black horsemen filled or overwhelmed the world. گرد functions as a noun/adverbial ‘around/circuit’ in this poetic collocation.
segments.s257-g159: This is a compact, slightly elliptical poetic line. به پیش سپاه = “in front of/before the host”; اندرون پیل places ‘inside/within’ with پیل ‘elephant’ (an elephant coming into the inner ranks). شست is the past form of شستن “to wash” (here narrative past), describing an action associated with the elephant. Word order is compressed: locative/adverbial phrases lead, verb comes at the end.
segments.s257-g160: جهان پست گشته is a passive/resultative construction: گشته (perfect participle/“has become/been turned”) with پست ‘low/humbled’, so “the world has been humbled/laid low.” ز پیلان مست gives the cause: “because of/by the elephants (پیلان) drunk/intoxicated (مست),” where مست is used figuratively of the elephants’ staggering or tumultuous behaviour.
segments.s257-g145: Imperative clause with an implied subject 'you' commanding someone to send. جهاندیده‌ای is a descriptive noun/adjective ('one who has seen the world' / 'worldly‑wise') placed before the direction phrase; سوی + noun marks the destination ('toward/to the elders'). فرست is the (archaic/poetic) imperative 'send'. Word order is subject‑descriptor → direction → verb (imperative) which is common in Persian poetic style.
segments.s257-g146: Continuation of the imperatives: a short nominal phrase 'هشیوار' ('the alert/vigilant one') stands alone as the direct object (i.e. who to send). The item وز here is an archaic/formulaic connective (equivalent to و + ز or simply 'and') linking this command to the next; یادگیران is the destination/recipients ('learners, students'), and فرست repeats the imperative 'send'.
segments.s257-g147: بگشای is an imperative 'open' governing گوش ('ear/attention'); به + پند فراوانش means 'to his abundant advice' (به marks the target/recipient of attention). فراوانش ('its/his abundance') qualifies پند. The idiom 'گوش بگشای' = 'lend an ear / listen carefully'.
segments.s257-g148: Imperative verb بپوش ('cover/put on') takes چادر مهربانی ('a cloak/veil of kindness') as its object; بر او places that cloak over him ('upon him'). This is metaphorical: 'cover him with kindness.' Word order is preposition (بر او) → object (چادر مهربانی) → verb (imperative), typical for emphatic poetic phrasing.
segments.s257-g149: Imperative construction: داد کن ('do justice') is the command; به هر کار ('in every matter') sets the domain and با هر کسی ('with every person') states the addressee/partner. هر repeats to emphasize universality. داد is a noun 'justice' used with the verbal imperative کن to mean 'act justly.'
segments.s257-g150: Short admonition introduced by ز ('from/of' in poetic usage) marking origin/agent: ز یزدان ('from God' / 'by God'); نیکی دهش is a nominal phrase 'the goodness given' (دهش here as the gift/act of giving), and یاد کن is an imperative 'remember.' Together: 'Remember the goodness granted by God.'
segments.s257-g151: Narrative clause: چنین ('thus') introduces reported speech. گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (here the senses list supplied was semantic for 'conversation' rather than the verb, so the correct reading is the verb 'said'). سالار لشکر ('commander of the army') is the speaker and به شاه marks the addressee ('to the king'). Word order is adverbial → verb → subject → indirect object in a compact poetic form.
segments.s257-g152: Reported content clause introduced by که ('that'). فرمان تو ('your command') is the subject; برتر از ('higher/superior to') is the comparative structure, and شید و ماه (sun and moon) are the standards of comparison. The clause means 'that your decree/command surpasses even sun and moon' (a conventional hyperbolic compliment).
segments.s257-g105: بزن is an imperative verb placed before its objects (verb-first imperative). Here it commands to produce sound (instrumental action). کوس رویین places the noun کوس (war‑drum) before the adjective رویین (postpositive adjective ‘iron/tempered’), a typical classical Persian order. The conjunctions و simply link شیپور (trumpet) and نای (reed‑flute) as additional objects of the same imperative.
segments.s257-g106: به introduces a direction/extent ('to/through') and governs the following place names کشمیر و کابل. فزون here functions as an adverbial/comparative notion 'more/moreover' (i.e. cause an increase or extend the sounding), and زین مپای is a noun phrase where زین (saddle/trappings) is modified by the following adjective مپای (poetic/rare). Word order (noun then modifier) is poetic/classical.
segments.s257-g107: که introduces a purpose/result clause here ('so that, in order that'). ما را uses the pronoun 'we/us' plus the object marker را to mark the definite direct object. سر is the object ('head' = life/being), followed by the genitive phrase از جنگ افراسیاب ('from/of the war of Afrasiab'), so the whole phrase names what is to be protected from Afrasiab's war.
segments.s257-g108: نیابد is a classical negative optative/subjunctive (3rd singular) meaning 'may he not obtain/find' (here: 'may it not find'). همی is a classical present/progressive particle often rendered 'ever/indeed' and here emphasizes the verbal finding. خورد is used as a verbal predicate meaning 'eating' (i.e. 'found us eating'); آرام and خواب are nouns/adjectives ('at peace, resting' and 'sleep') coordinated by و, completing the list of states in which we must not be found. Grammatically, the verb نیابد takes a clause of states (خورد و آرام و خواب) as its complement.
segments.s257-g109: الانان و غزدژ appear as the things handed over; به لهراسب داد is the dative phrase plus the past transitive verb داد ('gave'). Classical Persian often places the direct object(s) before the indirect object and verb; here 'داد' is the past tense main verb, and به + لهراسب marks the recipient.
segments.s257-g110: بدو is the dative clitic phrase 'to him' (to that person); گفت is the past of 'to say' ('he said'). کای is a vocative particle ('O') addressing someone (or the royal title Kay); گرد here functions as an epithet attached to خسرو نژاد ('the valiant/hero of Khosrow's line' — گرد + [name/lineage] is a customary epic epithet), and نژاد is the noun 'lineage/descendant'. The sequence is a direct speech introduction: 'he said to him, "O ..., descendant of Khosrow".'
segments.s257-g111: برو is the imperative 'go'. با سپاهی is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with an army/with troops'. به کردار کوه is a simile phrase 'in manner/behavior like a mountain' (به + abstract noun کردار 'conduct/behavior' followed by the simile کوه), instructing firmness and steadiness. The adjective/simile follows کردار in typical Persian order.
segments.s257-g112: گزین کن is a two‑part imperative: گزین ('choose/select') followed by the auxiliary imperative کن ('do'), a common periphrastic command. ز is the poetic preposition 'from', introducing the source phrase گردان لشکر ('the army's regiments/divisions') and گروه ('a band/group') as the object to be chosen; word order places the source before the verb in classical style.
segments.s257-g17: گر is the conditional 'if' introducing the protasis; آید is 3rd person singular present (classical use indicating 'comes' or 'should come') governed by گر. به ایران is a prepositional phrase 'into/to Iran' (به = toward/into + ایران). به جنگ expresses the purpose 'to war/for battle' (به = to/for + جنگ = fight). آن سپاه is the noun phrase 'that host/army' with آن as the demonstrative determiner before the head noun سپاه.
segments.s257-g18: هژبر is a proper name (epithet), followed by the epithet دلاور 'the brave' in apposition. نیاید is the negative present/optative form (ن‑ + آید) meaning 'does not come / will not come' and here gives the apodosis to the conditional. به راه is idiomatic 'to the road / to the field' (به = toward; راه = road/way), i.e. 'take to the field.'
segments.s257-g19: سر مرز here means 'the head/chief of the frontier' (سر as 'chief/leader') and مرز توران is the genitive phrase 'the frontier of Turan.' به + پیران marks the indirect object 'to Piran' (to/for + Piran). سپرد is the past tense verb 'he entrusted / handed over.'
segments.s257-g20: سپاهی فرستاد is a simple past clause 'he sent soldiers/an army' (سپاهی = troops; فرستاد = sent). با او 'with him' uses با as the comitative preposition plus the pronoun او. نه خرد is a terse negative attribute 'without wisdom / not wise' modifying the troops; خرد = 'wisdom' (here 'lacking sense').
segments.s257-g21: سوی is the preposition 'toward(s)' and opens the locative phrase 'toward the frontier of Khwarezm.' پنجه هزار is an archaic numeral phrase ('پنجه' = old form of 'پنج' = five), i.e. 'five thousand'; here it quantifies the force. (Note: the segment's provided senses do not list a numeral sense for پنجه, so a numeric sense is indicated below as -1.)
segments.s257-g22: کمربسته is a participial/adjectival form 'girded (at the waist)' meaning 'armed/ready for action'; it functions adverbially with رفت 'went.' از در کارزار literally 'from the gate of the battle' (از = from/out of; در = gate/door; کارزار = battlefield), a common poetic way to say 'he went out to the battlefield.'
segments.s257-g23: سپهدارشان is سپهدار 'commander' with the possessive suffix -شان 'their' (i.e. 'their commander'). شیده is an adjectival/participial epithet meaning 'mad, frenzied' and precedes the compound شیر دل 'lion‑hearted' (شیر = lion used adjectivally + دل = heart), forming a string of epithets describing the commander.
segments.s257-g24: کز is the relative/connector 'who/which/that' introducing a relative clause modifying سپهدارشان. آتش here is the poetic 'fire' (often figurative 'ardor/passion') and ستاند is the verb 'stole/took/snatched' in classical usage (note: the provided lemma senses list for ستاند does not include this 'to seize/take' meaning, so a direct sense index is not available). به شمشیر uses به as the instrumental 'by/with' (here 'with the sword') and دل again is 'heart,' forming the figurative instrumental phrase 'with the sword of (his) heart.'
segments.s257-g137: کسی کو functions as a relative phrase 'someone who...'; کو here is an archaic/poetic relative connector (literally 'who/that') linking to the verb. به جنگت places the action with respect to 'your battle' (به + noun = 'in/for your battle'), and نبندد is a negative 3rd-person present/subjunctive-like form of بستن used here in the sense 'bind/join' (i.e., 'does not bind/attach himself'); میان is an adverb 'among/in the midst' specifying where he does not join.
segments.s257-g138: چنان ساز کش is idiomatic: چنان 'such' qualifying ساز 'instrument/call' and کش (here verbal/active) roughly 'play/sound' or 'set in motion' — together 'sound such a call' or 'make such an arrangement'. از تو ناید زیان is a small clause 'harm does not come from you' (ناید = 'does not come' here, i.e. 'will not befall'); the sequence gives the result/consequence: if the person won't join, do not make that call and you will suffer no loss.
segments.s257-g139: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that / since'); نپسندد is a negative-present/optative form of پسندیدن 'to approve/like' meaning 'does not approve'; از ما marks the source/agent 'from us'; بدی is the object 'evil/wrongdoing'; دادگر follows as the logical subject 'the Just One' (i.e. a just judge/ruler), placed after the clause for topical emphasis.
segments.s257-g140: سپنجست is an archaic/poetic adjective meaning 'uncertain/obscure' describing گیتی 'the world'; و simply links the next imperative or hortative, ما برگذر is a hortatory phrase 'and we (should) let it pass/overlook' (برگذر used in the sense 'let go, pardon, pass by').
segments.s257-g141: چو here = 'when'; لشکر سوی مرز توران is a straightforward prepositional phrase 'the army toward the border of Turan' (سوی = 'toward'); بری is not used in its common senses here (the given sense list doesn't match the poetic form), so it functions as part of the verbal/nominal phrase indicating movement/going in this older diction.
segments.s257-g142: مکن is the negative imperative 'do not'. تیز دل (adjective + noun) = 'eager/keen heart'; را is the definite/object marker attaching to دل. به introduces the target or instrument ('into/with'), and آتش سری is a genitive/nominal phrase 'secret fire' (آتش = fire, سری = secret) = hidden passion; the whole says 'do not inflame/drive your keen heart into secret fire.'
segments.s257-g143: نگر is an imperative 'look/consider'. تا introduces purpose/result 'so that'; نجوشی is a negative-jussive/present form of جوشیدن used metaphorically 'you do not seethe/become inflamed' (with anger); به کردار طوس literally 'at/regarding the conduct of Tûs' (به + noun phrase marking the object of emotional reaction).
segments.s257-g144: نبندی is a negative jussive/imperative form 'do not bind/attach (yourself)'; به هر کار = 'to every undertaking' (به + هر + کار); بر is the preposition 'on/at' and پیل کوس is a compound image 'elephant-drum' (پیل 'elephant' modifying کوس 'war-drum') — the advice: do not commit yourself to every enterprise at the sounding of the grand drum (i.e., at every boastful signal).
